vignette.title: The Case
vignette.text:
A 58-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by EMS after being awakened from sleep at midnight with severe chest pain. He describes the pain as a crushing pressure radiating down his left arm and into his jaw, associated with profuse diaphoresis, nausea, and shortness of breath. He rates the pain as 10/10.

Past Medical History: Hypertension (10 years), Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (8 years), Hyperlipidemia (5 years)
Medications: Lisinopril 20mg daily, Metformin 1000mg BID, Atorvastatin 40mg nightly
Family History: Father died of MI at age 52. Brother had CABG at age 55.
Social History: 30 pack-year smoking history.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash)
vignette.sourceNote: Clinical presentation per Universal Definition of MI [universal-mi-2018]

physicalExam.general: Alert, anxious, diaphoretic, in obvious distress
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular. S4 gallop. No murmurs.

ecg.description: ST elevation 3-4mm in V1-V4, 2mm in I/aVL. Reciprocal ST depression in II, III, aVF.

questions.stem: The ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1-V4 with reciprocal changes in II, III, and aVF.
questions.question: Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?
questions.explanation: ST elevation in V1-V4 indicates anterior wall involvement, supplied by the LAD. The LAD courses in the anterior interventricular groove and supplies the anterior LV wall and anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum.

whyChain.why: Why do V1-V4 indicate anterior wall involvement?
whyChain.answer: Precordial leads V1-V4 overlie the anterior chest, directly over the anterior heart surface. They detect electrical changes in underlying tissue.

whyChain.why: Why does the LAD supply the anterior wall?
whyChain.answer: The LAD arises from the left main and courses down the anterior interventricular groove. Diagonal branches supply the anterior wall; septal perforators supply the septum.

questions.stem: Understanding conduction system blood supply is critical for anticipating complications.
questions.question: The AV node receives blood supply from which artery in ~90% of patients?
questions.explanation: The AV nodal artery arises from the RCA in ~90% of people (right-dominant circulation) and from LCx in ~10%. This is why inferior MIs cause AV blocks more often than anterior MIs.

questions.stem: The patient reports pain radiating to his left arm and jaw.
questions.question: Which mechanism explains cardiac referred pain to the arm and jaw?

options.text: Direct innervation of the arm by cardiac sympathetic fibers
Convergence of cardiac visceral afferents and somatic afferents at spinal cord T1-T4
Vagus nerve carrying pain to both heart and arm

questions.explanation: Cardiac pain travels via visceral afferents to T1-T4 spinal segments, where they converge with somatic afferents from the arm (brachial plexus C5-T1) and jaw. The brain cannot distinguish the source.

questions.stem: An S4 gallop is heard on cardiac auscultation.
questions.question: The S4 heart sound is produced by which event?

options.text: Atrial contraction forcing blood into a stiff, non-compliant left ventricle
Rapid passive filling of the ventricle in early diastole
Turbulent flow across a stenotic aortic valve
Closure of the mitral and tricuspid valves

questions.explanation: S4 occurs in late diastole when the atrium contracts against a stiff ventricle. In acute ischemia, impaired relaxation (diastolic dysfunction) makes the ventricle non-compliant.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does ischemia cause ventricular stiffness?
whyChain.answer: Relaxation is ATP-dependent (SERCA pump requires ATP for calcium reuptake). Ischemia depletes ATP, impairing calcium sequestration and causing persistent actin-myosin interaction.

questions.stem: The LAD supplies critical structures beyond the anterior wall.
questions.question: In proximal LAD occlusion, which structure is MOST at risk?
questions.explanation: The LAD gives off septal perforators supplying the anterior two-thirds of the septum. Proximal occlusion causes septal infarction, leading to conduction abnormalities and possibly septal rupture.

whyChain.why: Why does septal involvement worsen prognosis?
whyChain.answer: The septum contains the bundle of His and branches. Infarction causes conduction blocks (RBBB, LBBB). The septum also contributes significantly to LV function.

correct.clinicalPearl: The LAD gives off septal perforators supplying the anterior two-thirds of the septum. Proximal occlusion causes septal infarction, leading to conduction abnormalities and possibly septal rupture.

questions.stem: The patient's heart rate is 102 bpm despite severe distress.
questions.question: What is the PRIMARY mechanism driving tachycardia in this patient?

options.text: Direct effect of ischemia on SA node automaticity
Baroreceptor reflex responding to decreased cardiac output

questions.explanation: Ischemia reduces contractility → decreased stroke volume → decreased CO → decreased BP → baroreceptor unloading → sympathetic activation → tachycardia and vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: Blood pressure is 158/98 despite acute cardiac injury.
questions.question: What explains elevated blood pressure during acute MI?

options.text: Increased cardiac output from Starling mechanism
Acute kidney injury causing volume retention
Intense sympathetic vasoconstriction outweighing decreased cardiac output

questions.explanation: Massive sympathetic activation causes intense arterial vasoconstriction (α1 effect), increasing SVR. Since MAP ≈ CO × SVR, marked SVR increase can maintain BP despite reduced CO.

questions.stem: Coronary perfusion timing is critical for understanding ischemia.
questions.question: During which phase does the LEFT ventricle receive most coronary blood flow?
questions.explanation: During systole, LV contraction compresses intramyocardial vessels, impeding flow. During diastole, relaxation relieves compression, and flow occurs. Tachycardia shortens diastole, reducing perfusion time.

questions.stem: The Frank-Starling mechanism attempts compensation.
questions.question: How does the Frank-Starling mechanism compensate for reduced contractility?

options.text: Increased preload stretches functional myocytes, increasing their contraction force
Increased heart rate maintains output despite lower stroke volume
Blood flow redistribution away from non-essential organs

questions.explanation: When ischemic myocardium hypocontracts, blood accumulates → increased LVEDV → stretches remaining functional myocytes → enhanced contraction of healthy segments (Frank-Starling law).

whyChain.why: Why does stretch increase contractile force?
whyChain.answer: Moderate stretch optimizes actin-myosin overlap and increases troponin C calcium sensitivity. However, excessive stretch moves sarcomeres past optimal overlap, causing force to decrease.

questions.stem: Understanding the cause of STEMI is essential for treatment.
questions.question: What is the MOST common proximate cause of acute STEMI?

options.text: Rupture or erosion of a vulnerable atherosclerotic plaque with superimposed thrombus

questions.explanation: Most STEMIs result from rupture/erosion of a vulnerable plaque (thin cap, large lipid core, inflammatory cells), exposing thrombogenic contents, triggering platelet adhesion and coagulation cascade activation.

options.text: Release of reactive oxygen species from mitochondria
Activation of apoptotic pathways via caspases

options.text: Subendocardium has highest concentration of coronary arteries

questions.question: What is aspirin's mechanism of benefit in ACS?

options.text: Dissolution of fibrin clot via plasminogen
Irreversible COX-1 inhibition blocking thromboxane A2 and platelet aggregation

Blocking pain receptors in cardiac afferents
Nitric oxide release causing venodilation (decreased preload) and coronary vasodilation

questions.stem: The wife asks about family risk.
questions.question: Which response BEST integrates genetic and environmental concepts?

options.text: Only males are at risk due to Y-chromosome inheritance.
Heart disease is entirely genetic; each child has 50% chance of MI at same age.
Familial clustering suggests inherited susceptibility, but children can substantially modify risk through lifestyle and preventive care.
Family history is coincidental; MIs are random events.

questions.explanation: Premature CAD has strong genetic components (familial hypercholesterolemia, Lp(a), inflammatory variants), but is polygenic and multifactorial. Genes create susceptibility; environment modifies expression. Lifestyle reduces risk ~50% even with high genetic risk.

keyTakeaways.point: ACS results from vulnerable plaque rupture triggering thrombosis—not gradual stenosis
Wavefront of necrosis progresses subendocardium to epicardium over 3-6 hours
Door-to-balloon ≤90 minutes for PCI; door-to-needle ≤30 minutes for fibrinolytics
Aspirin reduces vascular mortality by 23%—give immediately
Troponin release indicates irreversible myocyte injury with membrane disruption
LVEF is strongest predictor of long-term mortality

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of acute coronary syndrome. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.explanation: LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.
Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.

ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.
LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
